message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "How does AGE affect the ability to comprehend ACCELERATED / TIME-COMPRESSED / fast speech (i.e. "speed listening")? Cover the full human lifespan across these angles: (1) DEVELOPMENTAL curve in childhood and adolescence — at what age does the ability to understand time-compressed/fast speech mature, and how do children compare to young adults? (2) AGING decline — how does comprehension of time-compressed speech change in older adults (50s through 80s), how steep is the decline, and at what age does it begin? Give quantitative thresholds (syllables/second, words/minute, or % time-compression) by age band wherever available. (3) MECHANISMS — disentangle peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis) from central/cognitive factors: central auditory temporal processing, cognitive/perceptual processing speed, and working-memory / phonological-loop capacity; which factor dominates the age effect, and does the age penalty persist after controlling for audibility? (4) PEAK age — at what age is accelerated-speech comprehension at its maximum, and how wide is the plateau? (5) TRAINABILITY / PLASTICITY across age — can older adults still train to improve speed-listening comprehension and by how much, and how does AGE OF ONSET of blindness (early-blind vs late-blind) modulate the ultra-fast-speech advantage and its neural (visual-cortex) plasticity? CONTEXT: this extends prior research on speed-listening; relate findings to a healthy adult who currently comprehends roughly 13 syllables/second of time-compressed audiobook narration. Prefer peer-reviewed sources; provide quantitative figures and effect sizes by age group where possible, and flag where peripheral hearing loss is or isn't controlled for."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5399767/
**Title:** Verbal Working Memory in Older Adults: The Roles of Phonological Capacities and Processing Speed (J Speech Lang Hear Res, 2017)
**Found via:** Cognitive processing speed & working-memory/phonological-loop mediators search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract claims about how aging affects verbal working memory, phonological capacities, and processing speed. Specifically: (1) Does age reduce verbal working memory, and by how much? (2) What is the relative role of phonological processing capacity vs cognitive/perceptual processing speed in explaining age-related decline in verbal working memory? Which factor dominates? (3) Any quantitative figures, effect sizes, correlations, or path coefficients linking processing speed and phonological loop capacity to working memory across age. (4) Was peripheral hearing loss measured or controlled for? (5) Sample size, age range/bands, study design. (6) Publication date and authors. Provide direct verbatim quotes from the article for each point.

content.content:
# Study Summary: Aging, Verbal Working Memory, and Processing Speed

**Publication & Authors:** Nittrouer et al., Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing Research, December 2016

**Sample:** "20 were between 18 and 32 years of age, and 25 were between 60 and 80 years of age"

---

## (1) Age-Related Decline in Verbal Working Memory

**Digit Span:** No significant difference. "Forward and reverse digit spans were similar across groups."

**Serial Recall:** Significant decline in older adults. "Accuracy on the serial recall task was poorer for older than for younger adults" with effect size η² = .206-.281 depending on list length.

---

## (2) Relative Roles: Processing Speed vs. Phonological Capacity

**Processing Speed Dominates:** "Slowing in processing abilities accounts for the primary trajectory of age-related declines in verbal working memory."

**Phonological Capacity Secondary:** "However, individual differences in phonological capacities explain variability among individual older adults." Older adults showed equivalent phonological abilities to younger adults on all three phonological tasks.

---

## (3) Quantitative Links

**Covariate Analysis:** When response time was used as a covariate, the age effect in accuracy was "reduced but not necessarily eliminated: for eight-item lists, F(1, 40) = 5.01, p = .031, η² = .111; for six-item lists, F(1, 40) = 3.05, p = .089."

**Within-Older-Adults Regression:** For six-item lists, "performance on the backwards words task...explained a significant amount of variance, standardized β = .410, p = .042" and "response time, standardized β = −.357, p = .046."

---

## (4) Hearing Loss Measurement & Control

**Yes, controlled:** "Auditory thresholds for the octave frequencies between 250 Hz and 8000 Hz were measured, and better-ear pure-tone averages (PTAs) were computed." Inclusion criterion: "thresholds at 2000 Hz needed to be better than 45-dB hearing level." All participants "reported negative histories of speech, language, and hearing problems" and "none used amplification."

---

## (5) Study Design Details

**Two sessions**, randomized task order, mixed-model ANOVAs with repeated measures on condition/position and between-subjects age factor. Younger group: college students; older group: "all participants in the older adult group had taken at least some college courses, and most had 4-year degrees."

---

## (6) Direct Conclusion Quote

"The results of the experiment presented here confirm the highly interactive nature of language processing and cognition, especially as they relate to normal aging."

content.text: Based on the WebFetch results, I have sufficient information to extract the key claims. This is a peer-reviewed primary research article from the Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing Research.

claims.claim: Cognitive/perceptual processing speed (slowing) is the dominant factor driving age-related decline in verbal working memory, while phonological capacities account for individual variability among older adults rather than the primary age trajectory.
claims.quote: Slowing in processing abilities accounts for the primary trajectory of age-related declines in verbal working memory. However, individual differences in phonological capacities explain variability among individual older adults.

claims.claim: When processing speed (response time) was statistically controlled as a covariate, the age effect on serial-recall accuracy was reduced but not fully eliminated, indicating processing speed mediates but does not exhaust the age penalty.
claims.quote: for eight-item lists, F(1, 40) = 5.01, p = .031, η² = .111; for six-item lists, F(1, 40) = 3.05, p = .089

claims.claim: Older adults (60-80) showed significantly poorer serial-recall accuracy than younger adults (18-32), with effect sizes η² = .206-.281, even though forward and reverse digit spans were equivalent across age groups.
claims.quote: Accuracy on the serial recall task was poorer for older than for younger adults

claims.claim: Peripheral hearing was measured and controlled: pure-tone thresholds from 250-8000 Hz were obtained, an inclusion threshold of better than 45-dB HL at 2000 Hz was required, and no participant used amplification, so the residual age effect on verbal working memory persists after audibility screening.
claims.quote: Auditory thresholds for the octave frequencies between 250 Hz and 8000 Hz were measured, and better-ear pure-tone averages (PTAs) were computed.

claims.claim: Within older adults, both phonological capacity (backwards-words task) and processing speed (response time) independently predicted six-item serial recall, showing phonological-loop and speed contributions are separable.
claims.quote: performance on the backwards words task...explained a significant amount of variance, standardized β = .410, p = .042 and response time, standardized β = −.357, p = .046

input.publishDate: 2016-12 (Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing Research, 2017 issue; Nittrouer et al.)

content.text:
Extracted 5 falsifiable claims from this primary peer-reviewed source (Nittrouer et al., J Speech Lang Hear Res, 2016/2017). 

Key findings relevant to the research question's MECHANISMS angle: the study disentangles processing speed from phonological-loop capacity in older adults (60-80) vs young adults (18-32), finding processing speed (slowing) is the dominant driver of age-related verbal working memory decline while phonological capacity explains individual variation. Critically, peripheral hearing was screened/controlled (PTA, 45-dB HL threshold at 2000 Hz, no amplification users), and the age penalty on serial recall persisted even after controlling for audibility and after using response time as a covariate (reduced but not eliminated). This supports the "central/cognitive factors persist beyond peripheral hearing loss" thread of the speed-listening research.

Note on scope: this study used serial recall and digit-span tasks, not time-compressed speech directly, so its relevance is mechanistic/mediating rather than a direct measure of accelerated-speech comprehension.
